sector: Industrials — Building Products & Equipment (commercial HVAC, controls, fire & security, data-center thermal)
bull_thesis:
Johnson Controls is a transformed, focused buildings franchise riding the AI data-center thermal wave with a credible new margin engine. Three factors anchor the case.

First, demand is real and accelerating. Orders rose 30% organically in the fiscal second quarter of 2026, and the backlog hit a record $20.0 billion, up 26% organically from $18 billion and 20% growth in the prior quarter. Management says the gains are driven by data-center work, including new large-scale chiller platforms and a collaboration with NVIDIA to have Johnson Controls' equipment written into the thermal specifications for hyperscaler and sovereign-AI campuses. Management describes the company as 'architecting the thermal backbone for the next generation of AI computing.'

Second, margins are inflecting in a way that looks structural rather than temporary. Adjusted operating margin reached 15.5%, up 310 basis points (3.10 percentage points) year over year in the second quarter, after a 190-basis-point gain in the first quarter. The expansion is happening across all three geographic segments: the Americas at 19.5%, Europe/Middle East/Africa at 14.9% (up 370 basis points), and Asia-Pacific at 19.8% (up 350 basis points). The driver is the operating system introduced by chief executive Joakim Weidemanis, who came from Danaher and brought that company's discipline around simplification and lean operations. Adjusted earnings per share rose 45% year over year.

Third, capital discipline has arrived. The $8.1 billion sale of the residential and light-commercial HVAC business to Bosch, which closed in August 2025, generated roughly $5.0 billion in net cash and funded a $5.0 billion accelerated share buyback that is already completed. Net debt fell to 2.0 times trailing earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization (from 2.4 times), and free-cash-flow conversion is running near 100%. Full-year fiscal 2026 guidance was raised to roughly $4.85 in adjusted earnings per share, about 6% organic revenue growth, and approximately 50% operating leverage.

The competitive advantage rests on a large installed base of equipment, high-margin service contracts tied to that base, the OpenBlue controls platform, and specifications designed into data-center projects — recurring revenue that compounds as the backlog converts. If 70% of the $20 billion backlog converts within 12 months as management projects, and margins advance into the high-teens, both earnings power and the valuation multiple support a price in the $165–$185 range. Johnson Controls is on the winning side of the AI build-out and the broader electrification and building-efficiency retrofit trend, with a demand base spanning data centers, life sciences, pharmaceuticals, and research campuses that is more durable than a pure bet on AI capital spending.
bear_thesis:
The good news is already in the stock, and the most important remaining catalyst depends on the same single factor — hyperscaler AI capital spending — that drives the entire data-center theme. Four problems stand out.

First, there is a large gap between orders and revenue. Orders are up 30% and the backlog is up 26%, yet full-year organic revenue guidance is only about 6%. Management itself flags that the backlog is 'longer-cycle,' that conversion 'depends on customer availability and ability to accept orders,' and that faster revenue 'won't come at operating leverage in the fifties.' The explosive AI demand is not current revenue; it is a deferred, customer-controlled option.

Second, the valuation offers no margin of safety (the gap between what you pay and what the business is worth). At roughly 24.75 times forward earnings and roughly 27 times enterprise value (the market cap plus net debt) divided by EBITDA (earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization), and near all-time highs of $147.75 on June 9, 2026, the stock already prices in successful backlog conversion and continued margin expansion. That EV/EBITDA multiple is richer than Trane Technologies at roughly 24.5 times, and far above Carrier Global at roughly 18 times, even though Johnson Controls is growing revenue more slowly than either peer's data-center exposure.

Third, pricing power is weaker on the biggest wins. Management conceded 'competitive bidding on larger orders' and shifted its language from 'pricing' to 'value-based engineering,' meaning the headline hyperscaler data-center equipment orders compete on price against Trane, Carrier, and Vertiv and may dilute the incremental margin on those wins.

Fourth, the key catalyst has largely already fired. The order and backlog inflection, the Bosch divestiture and share buyback, and the margin re-rating have all happened and are reflected in a stock at all-time highs. What remains live is a soft, customer-controlled, serially deferred backlog-conversion catalyst that hinges on the single shared AI capital-spending factor already underpinning the rest of the theme. A pause or slowdown in hyperscaler capital budgets — the single biggest risk to 2026 S&P 500 earnings growth — would slow conversion and compress a high-multiple, mid-single-digit-grower's stock sharply. Conservative downside to a fair roughly 22 times earnings is the mid-$120s to $115.
sop_bull: Bull valuation: $172–$185 per share, based on whole-company intrinsic value of the integrated post-Bosch franchise rather than a sum-of-the-parts (valuing each business line separately) breakdown, since the three geographic segments now operate as a single buildings platform. Bull-case adjusted earnings per share of roughly $5.50 in fiscal 2027 assumes the backlog converts and margins reach 16.5–17%. Applying a premium multiple of roughly 31–33 times (toward Trane's quality multiple, justified by accelerating orders, a clean balance sheet, and improving returns on invested capital) yields roughly $170–$182. The completed $5.0 billion accelerated share buyback shrinks the share count, adding another $5–$8 per share in accretion. A regional cross-check supports the same range: the Americas segment (data-center and service-heavy, running at 19.5% operating margin) is the crown jewel and would command a standalone premium; Europe/Middle East/Africa is inflecting (up 370 basis points); Asia-Pacific is the fastest-growing region (up 16%). Together these support $172–$185.
sop_bear: Bear valuation: $112–$122 per share. Conservative whole-company assumption: the backlog converts slowly and AI capital spending pauses, so fiscal 2027 adjusted earnings per share holds at roughly $4.85–$5.00 with no acceleration and margins plateau around 15.5%. Re-rating the stock to a cyclical-industrial multiple of roughly 22–24 times earnings (toward Carrier's roughly 18 times EV/EBITDA band, reflecting that the data-center equipment business is price-competitive and organic growth is only about 6%) yields roughly $110–$120. Net debt at 2.0 times is benign — there is no balance-sheet risk — and the $5 billion buyback provides a price floor, but neither rescues the stock if the multiple compresses. Stripping the AI-thermal premium and valuing the integrated franchise as a mid-single-digit commercial-buildings compounder at mid-teens EV/EBITDA on roughly $5 billion in EBITDA cross-checks to the same $112–$122 range.
sop_breakdown: Base case: roughly $140–$150 per share, with a midpoint near $145. The framework is whole-company intrinsic value on the integrated post-Bosch franchise. Fiscal 2027 adjusted earnings per share of roughly $5.20 assumes steady 6–7% organic growth, about 50% operating leverage, and share-count reduction from the buyback. Applying a multiple of 27–28 times — a justified premium to Carrier's roughly 23.6 times forward earnings and a modest discount to Trane's roughly 29–30 times, reflecting Johnson Controls' improving but still narrower competitive advantage and slower organic growth than Trane — yields roughly $140–$145. Two cross-checks: (a) EV/EBITDA — roughly $5.0–$5.3 billion in EBITDA at 25–26 times (between Carrier at 18 times and Trane at 24.5 times, edging above Trane on order momentum) implies enterprise value of roughly $130 billion less roughly $9 billion in net debt, or roughly $148 per share; (b) the $5 billion executed buyback and 2.0 times leverage add a few dollars of price floor. By region: the Americas (data-center and service crown jewel at 19.5% operating margin) represents the majority of value; Europe/Middle East/Africa (inflecting, 14.9%, up 370 basis points) and Asia-Pacific (fastest-growing, 19.8%, up 350 basis points) provide the growth kicker. The base-case result of roughly $145 sits essentially at the roughly $147 market price, which means the AI-thermal optionality is substantially priced in.
risk_reward: The risk/reward is poor and roughly symmetric at roughly $147 per share. Upside to the base case is negative 1% to positive 2% (fair value roughly $145). Upside to the bull case is roughly 17–26% ($172–$185) but requires successful backlog conversion and margins advancing into the high-teens. Downside to the bear case is roughly negative 17–24% ($112–$122) on an AI capital-spending pause. That works out to approximately 1-to-1 risk/reward at the current price — the asymmetry is gone because the stock ran into the catalyst. A better entry would be on a hyperscaler-capex flush toward the mid-$120s, which would restore a more attractive roughly 2-to-1 upside-to-downside ratio.
catalyst_status: SOFT_EXTENDED / partially FIRED. The load-bearing catalysts have largely ALREADY FIRED and are reflected: (1) the orders/backlog inflection — +30% orders, $20.0B backlog (+26%) — is a realized, dated print (Q2 FY26 release May 6, 2026), not a forward event; the stock is at/near all-time highs ($147.75 Jun 9, 2026) having already run it. (2) The Bosch $8.1B R&LC divestiture CLOSED Aug 1, 2025 and the $5.0B ASR is EXECUTED — fully FIRED. (3) The FY26 guidance raise (to ~$4.85) is done. What remains LIVE is the SOFT, customer-controlled, serially-deferred BACKLOG-CONVERSION catalyst — '70% of $20B converts within 12 months,' but management explicitly conditions it on hyperscaler 'customer availability and ability to accept orders,' i.e., a non-binding, third-party-dependent, single-shared-factor (AI-capex) driver. There is NO hard-dated binding catalyst (no PDUFA, no NRC milestone, no fixed deal terms). Per the regime doc, an already-fired/already-ran catalyst on the same shared AI-capex factor as the rest of the basket is PENALIZED.
peer_comps_note:
True peers within the data-center thermal segment, as verified in mid-June 2026. Trane Technologies trades at roughly 29–30 times forward earnings and roughly 24.5 times EV/EBITDA; it is the highest-quality data-center thermal pure-play, growing the fastest, and commands the richest multiple. Carrier Global trades at roughly 23.6 times forward earnings and roughly 18.1 times EV/EBITDA; it is the cheapest of the group, post-portfolio restructuring, with a lower data-center mix. Vertiv, the highest AI-sensitivity name in power and liquid cooling, trades at a large growth premium (roughly 30–35 times or more forward earnings) on much faster growth and is the purest AI capital-spending play in the segment. Johnson Controls itself trades at roughly 24.75 times forward earnings and roughly 27 times EV/EBITDA on roughly 6% organic growth and an adjusted operating margin of 15.5% and rising.

On valuation: Johnson Controls sits in the middle of the pack on the price-to-earnings multiple (above Carrier, below Trane) but is the most expensive on EV/EBITDA at roughly 27 times — richer than Trane at 24.5 times and far above Carrier at 18 times — which is a stretch given it is the slowest organic revenue grower of the group. On growth: Johnson Controls' order growth (up 30%) is best-in-class, but converted revenue (roughly 6% organic) trails Trane and Vertiv; Vertiv leads, then Trane, then Johnson Controls roughly in line with Carrier, on near-term top-line growth. On margins: Johnson Controls is improving fastest off a lower starting base (up 310 basis points); Trane is structurally highest; Johnson Controls is now closing the gap, with Carrier in the middle. On returns on invested capital: Johnson Controls has historically been mediocre at roughly 7–8% but is rising roughly 3–4 percentage points per year; Trane remains best-in-class. On AI capital-spending exposure: Vertiv is highest (power and cooling together), followed by Trane and Johnson Controls (thermal and controls), with Carrier lower. Net assessment: Johnson Controls is a quality-improving number-three in the peer group trading at a full multiple. Its EV/EBITDA premium to Trane is not justified by growth and is defensible only on the order-momentum and margin-inflection story.
role_in_scaleout: Johnson Controls supplies the data-center thermal backbone — large-scale chillers (its YDAM and YKHT platforms), computer-room air handlers, controls via the OpenBlue platform, and integrated build-out systems for hyperscaler and sovereign-AI campuses, including a position inside NVIDIA's thermal reference architecture for AI facilities. The demand is durable because cooling is non-optional for every AI campus and the service and controls contract tied to the installed equipment is sticky for the life of the asset. However, the marquee equipment orders on large data-center projects compete head-to-head on price against Trane, Carrier, and Vertiv, so Johnson Controls captures more value through the recurring service moat than through premium equipment pricing.
consensus_delta: There is little exploitable disagreement with the market consensus left. Wall Street has already caught up to the AI-thermal story, which is precisely the problem. Consensus has bid Johnson Controls to roughly 24.75 times forward earnings and roughly 27 times EV/EBITDA at all-time highs ($147.75 on June 9, 2026) explicitly for the data-center backlog. The thesis is consensus, not contrarian. The only genuine analytical wedge is the orders-to-revenue conversion bridge: bulls extrapolate the 30% order growth into revenue acceleration, while management itself guides only roughly 6% organic growth and warns that conversion is customer-controlled and long-cycle ('a lot depends on customer availability and ability to accept orders'). If the market is making an error, it is over-discounting near-term conversion — pricing the $20 billion backlog as if it converts on Johnson Controls' timetable when it actually converts on the hyperscalers'. That is a downside risk to consensus expectations, not a hidden upside opportunity. There is no mispriced good news here; expectations are full.
valley_of_death: There is no balance-sheet stress window: net debt is a benign 2.0 times EBITDA, free-cash-flow conversion is running near 100%, there is no near-term debt maturity wall, and the Bosch proceeds are in with the buyback already executed. The real 3-to-9-month risk is a multiple compression event rather than a cash crunch. Four triggers could cause it: (1) The single biggest regime-level risk is a pause in AI capital spending — hyperscaler capital expenditure represents roughly 40% of 2026 S&P 500 earnings growth, so any guide-down from a major cloud provider or a data-center digestion scare would slow Johnson Controls' backlog conversion and compress a stock trading at 27 times EV/EBITDA simultaneously, a double hit toward the mid-$120s. (2) The gap between orders and revenue means a quarter where order growth decelerates from the 30% pace (a tough comparison against that base) could puncture the narrative even if margins remain strong. (3) Pricing concessions on large data-center orders ('competitive bidding') could show up as softer incremental margins on the marquee wins. (4) The overall market is complacently positioned (a widely followed fund manager survey shows cash at 3.9%; the largest technology stocks represent roughly 30% of the index), leaving little dry powder to absorb an AI capital-spending shock, so a forced de-risking would hit AI-adjacent industrial stocks hard and indiscriminately.
positioning_washout: There is no forced-selling setup here. Johnson Controls pays a stable, well-covered dividend ($244 million per quarter) and its shareholder base is composed of quality-industrial and index investors, not yield-dependent holders under pressure to sell. The mechanical dynamic actually runs the other way: the completed $5.0 billion accelerated share buyback is a steady, price-insensitive buyer that shrinks the float and supports the stock, removing any capitulation setup. That is a negative for an aggressive entry — there is no flush of forced sellers to buy into; the stock is well-owned and well-bid. A genuine forced-selling wave would require an external AI capital-spending shock to trigger broad de-risking across crowded AI-adjacent industrial positions; absent that, there is no mechanical capitulation to exploit.
forcing_function: The only events that can force a re-rating are the quarterly earnings releases, and they cut both ways. The next fiscal quarter (third quarter, due roughly early August 2026) is the dated window where the market re-tests the orders-to-revenue conversion thesis. A print showing the $20 billion backlog beginning to inflect actual revenue — not just orders — would force analysts to upgrade estimates. Conversely, order growth decelerating off the 30% comparison base, or guidance that holds organic growth at roughly 6% despite the backlog size, would puncture the AI-conversion narrative. There is no hard, binding corporate event pending — no spin-off, no deal close, no regulatory decision — so the re-rating is gated entirely on hyperscaler capital-spending behavior showing up in Johnson Controls' conversion numbers, which is external to management's control.
moderator_conclusion:
Verdict: pass for now, conviction 2 out of 5. Johnson Controls is a genuinely strengthening, high-quality franchise — the analysis (management credibility 4 of 5; trajectory turning; moat narrow but real; moat trend widening; secular threat manageable) is correct and corroborated: orders are accelerating (up 30%), the balance sheet is clean and de-levered (2.0 times), the Danaher-style operating system is compounding real margin gains (up 310 basis points), and shareholder returns are funded by a completed divestiture. As a business, this is one of the better names in the data-center build-out theme. But as an entry at roughly $147, the case fails the investment discipline test on three counts.

First, the load-bearing catalyst has largely already fired. The order inflection, the Bosch divestiture and buyback, and the guidance raise have all happened and the stock is at all-time highs having run through them; already-fired catalysts are penalized under the investment framework.

Second, the remaining live catalyst — backlog conversion — is soft, customer-controlled, serially deferred, and hinges on the single shared AI capital-spending factor that the entire data-center theme already depends on. Adding Johnson Controls concentrates the portfolio in the same tail risk.

Third, valuation offers no margin of safety. Roughly 27 times EV/EBITDA is richer than Trane (24.5 times) despite slower organic growth, and the base-case valuation of roughly $145 sits essentially at the market price, leaving roughly 1-to-1 risk/reward.

The AI-thermal optionality is substantially priced in. The right move is to wait for an AI capital-spending flush toward the mid-$120s — where the risk/reward returns to roughly 2-to-1 and the franchise quality can be bought at a discount — rather than chasing it at all-time highs into the most-crowded factor in the market. Standalone value conviction is 3 of 5: stripping away the catalyst and timing overlay, the franchise quality, improving returns, clean balance sheet, and near-100% free-cash-flow conversion are real and the valuation says fairly valued (not expensive on a deteriorating base, not a value trap). But at full price with no discount to intrinsic value, the standalone value case is only fair, capped at 3 by the absence of a margin of safety.
interrogator_dossier:
Interrogator Forensic Dossier — Johnson Controls International (JCI)

Scale-out Basket C (Thermal). Lens: the physical bottlenecks of the AI compute build-out — specifically, data-center cooling and thermal management plus the controls and construction systems every hyperscaler campus must install. Prepared as Claude Opus 4.8, allocating real capital. Sources: second-quarter fiscal 2026 earnings release (May 6, 2026), first-quarter fiscal 2026 earnings transcript (February 4, 2026), fiscal 2025 full-year results, Bosch residential and light-commercial HVAC divestiture (closed August 1, 2025). Price roughly $147, market cap roughly $87–$90 billion as of mid-June 2026.

Narrative Arc

Johnson Controls has undergone a deliberate, multi-year transformation from a sprawling conglomerate-style buildings-and-batteries holding company into a focused commercial building solutions franchise, and the AI data-center thermal wave arrived at exactly the right moment. The arc has three legs. Portfolio purification culminated in the August 1, 2025 close of the $8.1 billion sale of the residential and light-commercial HVAC business to Bosch, generating roughly $5.0 billion in net cash. That deal removed the lower-margin consumer-cyclical business and re-cast Johnson Controls as a mission-critical commercial buildings, controls, and service company re-segmented into Americas, Europe/Middle East/Africa, and Asia-Pacific. Management reset followed: new chief executive Joakim Weidemanis, who came from Danaher, brought that company's operating discipline of simplify, accelerate, and amplify, shifting the narrative from portfolio strategy to execution and self-help margin improvement. The AI-thermal overlay completed the picture: 'architecting the thermal backbone for the next generation of AI computing,' a collaboration with NVIDIA on thermal reference architectures, and new large-scale chiller platforms; management consistently pairs the data-center story with life sciences, pharmaceuticals, and research campuses, signaling a desire for durability rather than pure AI capital-spending sensitivity. The story expanded and sharpened rather than drifting.

Claims Versus Financials

Demand described as AI and data-center-led: verified. Orders up 30% organically, backlog $20.0 billion up 26%, accelerating from $18 billion and 20% growth in the prior quarter. Margin described as structural: verified and accelerating. Adjusted operating margin 15.5% up 310 basis points versus the prior quarter's 12.4% up 190 basis points, expanding across all three regions — Americas 19.5%, Europe/Middle East/Africa 14.9% up 370 basis points, Asia-Pacific 19.8% up 350 basis points. Near-100% free-cash-flow conversion: verified (second-quarter free cash flow of $604 million, adjusted $526 million). De-levered balance sheet: verified at 2.0 times versus 2.4 times a year earlier. Capital return: verified, $5.0 billion accelerated buyback executed. Backlog conversion claim: in tension. Management projects 70% of the $20 billion converts within 12 months yet full-year organic revenue guidance is only roughly 6%; management itself flags that backlog is long-cycle and conversion 'depends on customer availability and ability to accept orders.' The gap between the 30% order growth and the 6% revenue guide is the central analytical tension — it is the honest physics of long-cycle data-center construction, but it caps near-term earnings flow-through.

Tone And Confidence Trajectory

Rising and more specific. Full-year 2026 guidance has been raised twice, to roughly $4.85 in adjusted earnings per share, roughly 6% organic growth, and roughly 50% operating leverage. Adjusted earnings per share up 45% year over year in the second quarter is a concrete delivered result. Management uses firmly offensive language ('thermal backbone') with disciplined caveats (China will not return to prior growth rates; backlog is long-cycle). The pattern of under-promising on revenue timing while over-delivering on margin is the inverse of a hype cycle.

Guidance Credibility

High. The pattern within fiscal 2026 is raise-and-beat. The deliberate refusal to guide revenue up to match the 30% order growth (holding the organic guide at roughly 6%) reads as disciplined sandbagging. Caveat: the new chief executive's track record at Johnson Controls spans only a few quarters. Reliability assessment: high.

Analyst Pressure Points

The orders-to-revenue conversion bridge is the recurring pressure point. The re-rating to revenue acceleration is deferred and customer-controlled, hinging on hyperscaler campus-readiness schedules. Secondary pressure points are pricing on large orders ('competitive bidding on larger orders'), China and Asia-Pacific durability, and production capacity to scale to the backlog. None has turned into bad news yet; conversion timing is the one most likely to disappoint if hyperscaler capital spending slows.

Red Flags And Green Flags

Positive signals: orders up 30% and backlog up 26% for two consecutive quarters; margin inflection across all regions (up 310 basis points); clean balance sheet (2.0 times) plus $5 billion executed buyback and raised guidance; purified mission-critical portfolio; credible Danaher-style operating system. Caution flags: orders-to-revenue gap (up 30% versus 6% — AI demand is a forward option, not current cash); competitive bidding on large data-center orders (weaker pricing power on the marquee wins); multiple already discounts the good news (roughly 25 times forward earnings and roughly 27 times EV/EBITDA at all-time highs); adjusted free cash flow below reported free cash flow (watch the reconciliation bridge).

Hidden Signals

Management deliberately diversifies the AI narrative by always pairing data-center wins with life sciences, pharmaceuticals, and research campuses — this is risk management, selling durability over pure AI sensitivity. The shift from 'pricing' to 'value-based engineering' signals that the company competes on total cost of ownership and embedded controls rather than price, conceding that marquee data-center equipment orders are price-competitive. The NVIDIA reference-architecture collaboration is a designed-in specification foothold. The new management team brings Danaher's operating DNA.

Capital-Allocation Assessment

Strengthening — a genuinely improving franchise with accelerating orders, a clean balance sheet, a credible operating system, a purified portfolio, and shareholder returns funded by the completed divestiture. The single thing to weigh before sizing: the stock already prices the good news. Roughly 25 times forward earnings and roughly 27 times EV/EBITDA on a roughly 6% organic grower bakes in successful conversion and continued margin expansion. The 30% order growth is a forward option on hyperscaler capital-spending durability — the same factor that drives the rest of the data-center theme — so the risk/reward is poor at this price.

Management credibility 4 of 5; trajectory strengthening; moat narrow but real; moat trend widening; secular threat manageable. Not a cheap fear-driven entry — a fairly-to-fully-valued quality compounder whose AI optionality is largely priced in.
skeptic_kill_fact: No clean disqualifying fact was found: every load-bearing figure verifies against the primary fiscal second-quarter 2026 release (SEC Form 8-K and press release, May 6, 2026) and the Bosch divestiture filing (August 1, 2025). The bear case — already-fired catalyst plus no margin of safety — is itself verified, not refuted, but it argues 'fairly valued at a poor entry point,' not a broken thesis. One factual error was found: the dossier and bull thesis both state 'Americas up 310 basis points,' but the Americas adjusted segment operating margin rose only roughly 100 basis points year over year (to 19.5%). The 310-basis-point figure is the total consolidated adjusted segment margin improvement to 15.5% — not an Americas-specific figure. Europe/Middle East/Africa up 370 basis points and Asia-Pacific up 350 basis points both verify. This does not break the overall case, but the per-region margin-inflection claim is overstated for the Americas, which management calls the crown jewel.
skeptic_corrections: First, Americas margin expansion is roughly 100 basis points year over year, not 310 basis points. The 310-basis-point improvement is the consolidated adjusted segment operating margin to 15.5%. Second, the claim that '70% of the backlog converts within 12 months' and the 'NVIDIA thermal reference-architecture collaboration' are not in the official earnings press release — they come from the earnings call transcript and investor-relations commentary, not the primary filing. Treat them as management-asserted rather than press-release-verified. Third, the Bosch net proceeds of roughly $5.0 billion after tax verify, but the headline $8.1 billion is the total deal value; JCI's gross consideration was roughly $6.7 billion. Use the net figure of roughly $5.0 billion for the buyback-funding math. Fourth, the current price is roughly $146 as of June 2026 and the all-time high of $147.75 on June 9, 2026 verifies. The base-case valuation of roughly $145 sits essentially at the market price, confirming roughly 1-to-1 risk/reward with the catalyst already fired. Size conviction accordingly.
skeptic_evidence: SEC Form 8-K / PR Newswire, 'Johnson Controls Reports Strong Q2 Results; Raises FY26 Guidance,' May 6 2026 — Q2 sales $6.1B, organic +6%, orders +30% organic, backlog $20.0B (+26% organic; Americas $14.9B / EMEA $3.2B / APAC $1.9B), adj EPS $1.19 (+45%), adj segment EBITA margin 15.5%, Americas +100bp/EMEA +370bp/APAC +350bp, net debt 2.0x, ~100% FCF conversion, FY26 guide raised to ~$4.85 adj EPS / ~6% organic / ~50% leverage.

Johnson Controls press release / SEC 8-K, 'Completes Sale of Residential and Light Commercial HVAC Business,' Aug 1 2025 — $8.1B total deal value, JCI consideration ~$6.7B, ~$5.0B net cash after tax, $5.0B accelerated share repurchase program announced.

Macrotrends / stockanalysis.com price history (accessed Jun 2026) — JCI all-time-high close $147.75 on Jun 9 2026; price ~$146, mkt cap ~$89B; forward P/E ~25-27x, EV/EBITDA ~22-28x (source-dependent) — full multiple at all-time highs, confirming the 'priced-in / no margin of safety' bear.

Q2 FY26 release reconciliation — orders +30% organic vs FY26 organic revenue guide only ~6%: the orders-to-revenue conversion gap is real and management-acknowledged, confirming the catalyst is a deferred, customer-controlled (hyperscaler-capex-gated) option, not current revenue.

scale.role: Building/HVAC controls with a DC backlog leg; orders +30%, backlog $20.0B (+26%).
scale.rationale: Quality re-rate (orders +30% organic, backlog $20.0B) but the DC leg is a minority of a broad buildings business — a TACTICAL-to-low-LEVER franchise. Skeptic verdict CONFIRMED_WITH_CORRECTIONS with a material per-region fix (Americas margin expanded +100bp not +310bp; the NVIDIA/thermal collaboration and 70%-conversion claims are transcript color, not in the release), so a ~10pt haircut off a ~54 franchise. Catalyst SOFT_EXTENDED/fired into all-time highs, base ~$145 ~at spot (~1:1); wait.
scale.demand_durability: Medium — building controls/HVAC, DC a growing but minority slice.
